clean_title: a note on numerical preconditions in softmax gradient translation-invariance testing
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf2.3.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.7.12 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version cudnn 8.6 gpu model and memory tesla v100 current behavior? hello tensorflow team, we’d like to report and clarify an observation from metamorphic testing of operator gradients, focusing on how large constant shifts interact with the numerical preconditions of certain metamorphic relations (mrs). we tested three operators under the same “constant shift” stress condition: avgpool2d (ap) maxpool2d (mp) softmax we observed that avgpool2d consistently satisfies the expected mr, while maxpool2d and softmax can exhibit reproducible deviations under large shifts. we have already reported the maxpool2d case separately; this email summarizes the broader finding and asks for guidance on how best to interpret/document it, especially for softmax. background (the mrs we tested) 1) constant shift transformation ( x' = x + c ) where (c) is constant along the mr-relevant axis (e.g., per-window constant for pooling, per-softmax slice constant for softmax), and may vary across other dimensions via broadcasting. 2) expected behavior avgpool2d: forward shifts by a constant; backward should be invariant under the same upstream gradient: ( gx' ≈ gx ) maxpool2d (with the precondition that argmax routing is preserved): ( gx' ≈ gx ) softmax (theoretically shift-invariant along the normalization axis): ( \mathrm{softmax}(x + c) = \mathrm{softmax}(x) ) and, under the same upstream gradient (g), ( gx' ≈ gx ) what we observed avgpool2d under large constant shifts (including magnitudes on the order of 1e4–1e5 in float32), avgpool2d did not show violations in our metamorphic checks (forward/gradient consistency remained within tolerance). maxpool2d under settings such as padding=same and stride=1, we observe reproducible mr violations with large violation metrics (vr) on both cpu and gpu when (|c|) is sufficiently large (e.g., hundreds or larger). this maxpool2d finding has already been reported. [url] softmax under large constant shifts (e.g., 1e4–1e5 in float32) applied as a constant along the softmax axis (via broadcasting), we observe reproducible numerical deviations in: forward output consistency and/or backward gradient consistency (gx) the effect appears implementation-path sensitive (e.g., cpu vs gpu and/or layout paths), though we can provide exact version/platform matrices and minimal repro scripts. our analysis / hypothesis we believe the maxpool2d behavior is expected under finite precision and ties: for large (|c|), float32 ulp becomes coarse relative to small differences. previously strictly ordered values can become equal or nearly equal after the shift (implicit ties). this can change argmax routing, hence backward routing changes and the mr precondition (“order preserved”) no longer holds. for softmax, we suspect a related but distinct numerical-stability / implementation-path-consistency issue: softmax is mathematically shift-invariant along the normalization axis, but under extreme magnitudes in float32, different kernel paths / reduction orders / stabilization behaviors can potentially lead to observable deviations. the fact that avgpool2d passes under the same stress condition suggests the phenomenon is not a general issue in the test harness, but operator-specific numerical behavior. questions we’d like to confirm the following points: for maxpool2d gradients, is it reasonable to treat violations under large constant shifts as not applicable (n/a) when the “argmax routing preserved” precondition is broken by finite-precision tie effects? for softmax, is it expected that extremely large constant shifts (float32) may lead to measurable forward/gradient deviations across device/layout implementations, or would you consider this worthy of investigation as an implementation-path consistency / numerical stability issue? would it be helpful to add a brief documentation note stating that large constant shifts can affect: maxpool gradient routing (due to finite-precision tie effects), and potentially softmax numerical consistency in extreme regimes, even though the underlying mathematical properties hold in real arithmetic? reproduction we have minimal deterministic scripts and concrete configurations (tf version, cpu/gpu, layout) for: maxpool2d (already reported), and softmax (new observation) we can provide them in this thread immediately if useful. thank you for your time and for the continued work on tensorflow. best regards, xiaohuaovo standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

clean_title: tensorflow 2.20.0 causes mutex crash in huggingface tokenizers on macos arm without being imported
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source binary tensorflow version 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution macos (apple silicon / arm64) - mac studio tahoe 26.1 mobile device no response python version 3.13.11 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.4.4.1) cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory apple silicon (mps backend) m2 ultra current behavior? simply having tensorflow 2.20.0 installed in a python environment causes a fatal mutex crash when using huggingface tokenizers, even though tensorflow is never imported in the code. when running code that loads a diffusers fluxpipeline (which uses huggingface tokenizers internally), the process crashes with: libc++abi: terminating due to uncaught exception of type std:: 1::system error: mutex lock failed: invalid argument this happens at the tokenizer loading stage, specifically when t5tokenizerfast is being initialized. expected behavior: tensorflow being installed but not imported should have no effect on unrelated libraries. the huggingface tokenizers library should load normally. key findings from debugging: 1. with tensorflow installed → crash occurs (tensorflow is never imported) 2. uninstall only tensorflow → works immediately 3. all tensorflow dependencies (keras, tensorboard, grpcio, h5py, etc.) can remain installed without issue 4. the issue is specifically the tensorflow package itself, not its dependencies 5. downgrading protobuf does not fix the issue 6. setting tokenizers parallelism=false does not fix the issue this suggests tensorflow 2.20.0 may be registering something at the native/shared library level that interferes with the rust-based tokenizers library's threading primitives on macos arm. standalone code to reproduce the issue [inline code] fluxpipeline [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] tensorflow [inline code] pip install torch torchvision diffusers transformers accelerate safetensors sentencepiece protobuf tensorflow 2. run the following python script: import torch from diffusers import fluxpipeline crash occurs during this call pipe = fluxpipeline.from pretrained( "black-forest-labs/flux.1-dev", torch dtype=torch.float16 ) pipe.to("mps") log output /users/username/venv-metal/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/amp/autocast mode.py:270: userwarning: user provided device type of 'cuda', but cuda is not available. disabling warnings.warn( loading pipeline components...: 0%| | 0/7 [00:00

clean_title: collectivegatherv2 tensorshape check failure
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution kali linux (kali-rolling) linux kali 6.11.2-amd64 mobile device no response python version python 3.10 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? executing tf.raw ops.collectivegatherv2 on a cpu-only vmware virtual machine causes a native crash inside tensorflow. before the crash, tensorflow prints a cuinit(303) message (expected for a cpu-only machine), and then immediately hits a fatal c++ check failure inside tensor shape.cc: 2025-11-21 08:11:23.906652: e external/local xla/xla/stream executor/cuda/cuda platform.cc:51] failed call to cuinit: internal: cuda error: failed call to cuinit: unknown error (303) 2025-11-21 08:11:23.925656: f tensorflow/core/framework/tensor shape.cc:587] check failed: d < dims() (0 vs. 0) zsh: iot instruction python q2.py there is no python exception. the entire python process aborts with an iot instruction (sigabrt) due to the failed check. this indicates a bug in the tensorshape handling logic inside collectivegatherv2. for scalar inputs (shape=[]), the kernel attempts to access dimension 0, producing the illegal condition 0 < 0. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

clean_title: tensorflow lite reference kernel reducesum overflows when network type not uint8
clean_body: system information - linux ubuntu 22.04 - tensorflow installed from pip source - tensorflow version 2.19.1 - tflite runtime installed from pip source - tflite runtime version 2.14.0 standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] the example network can be created from the attached file minimal.json. the network contains a single reducesum op, the input tensor has shape and type int8[1,128]. output is [[-1]] when it should be [[127]] [minimal.json]( [url] any other info / logs i have located the bug in the tensorflow lite reference kernel, in file /tensorflow/lite/kernels/reduce.cc. in the function quantizedmeanorsum, when kernel type is kreference, the call to reference ops::quantizedmeanorsum uses type uint8 instead of template type t. this results in overflow when a network has a different type. i have included the bug fix as a git patch below. [code block]
clean_text: tensorflow lite reference kernel reducesum overflows when network type not uint8 system information - linux ubuntu 22.04 - tensorflow installed from pip source - tensorflow version 2.19.1 - tflite runtime installed from pip source - tflite runtime version 2.14.0 standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] the example network can be created from the attached file minimal.json. the network contains a single reducesum op, the input tensor has shape and type int8[1,128]. output is [[-1]] when it should be [[127]] [minimal.json]( [url] any other info / logs i have located the bug in the tensorflow lite reference kernel, in file /tensorflow/lite/kernels/reduce.cc. in the function quantizedmeanorsum, when kernel type is kreference, the call to reference ops::quantizedmeanorsum uses type uint8 instead of template type t. this results in overflow when a network has a different type. i have included the bug fix as a git patch below. [code block]

clean_title: [bug] cuda error invalid ptx on new rtx 5080 gpu with driver 581.29
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source binary tensorflow version tensorflow 2.20.0 and 2.16.2 custom code yes os platform and distribution ubuntu 24.04.3 lts mobile device no response python version 3.11 bazel version n/a gcc/compiler version n/a cuda/cudnn version - cuda version (from nvidia-smi): 13.0 - cudnn version (installed via pip): 8.9.7 (from package nvidia-cudnn-cu12==8.9.7.29) - cuda libraries (installed via pip): compatible with cuda 12.x gpu model and memory nvidia geforce rtx 5080 laptop gpu, 16gb current behavior? current behavior when attempting to run any tensorflow operation on the gpu that requires jit compilation (e.g., tf.matmul), the execution fails with an internalerror. the detailed log output reveals that the root cause is a cuda error invalid ptx error, which occurs during the cumoduleloaddata step. this initial failure prevents the creation of a valid cuda function handle, leading to the subsequent and final cuda error invalid handle error when tensorflow tries to launch the kernel. this behavior is consistently reproducible on a new nvidia rtx 5080 laptop gpu with the latest drivers, and occurs with both tensorflow versions 2.20.0 and 2.16.2 installed from pip. key log line: expected behavior the expected behavior is that the tf.matmul operation should execute successfully on the gpu. specifically: tensorflow should correctly generate a valid ptx kernel for the new gpu architecture. the nvidia driver should accept this ptx code and successfully jit-compile it into native sass. the operation should complete without any internalerror, cuda error invalid ptx, or cuda error invalid handle exceptions. after the initial (and expected) jit compilation delay, subsequent calls to the same operation should be fast. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

clean_title: why does register replacement not support fuzzy matching for optional parameters in operators when using search fn or search fn pattern?
clean_body: i am attempting to write a pattern matcher to match fused operators, and currently encounter an issue: neither search fn nor search fn pattern provides a way to perform fuzzy matching for optional parameters. for example, optional parameters like offset and bias in the following operator prototype appear differently in the fx graph when they are passed or omitted: operator prototype as follows: [code block] case 1 (with optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1, offset=arg5 1, bias=arg6 1); case 2 (without optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1); attempt 1: using search fn the following search fn only matches case 1 and fails to match case 2 (where optional parameters are omitted). to cover case 2, i have to write a separate search fn: [code block] attempt 2: using search fn pattern (callfunction) i also tried using search fn pattern (from torch. inductor.pattern matcher import register replacement as register) and wrote a callfunction as shown below. however, using ignored() results in a placeholder , which causes a failure during the matching process when verifying len(self.args)!=len(node.args). it seems impossible to match both cases with a single callfunction: [code block] impact: some operators may have more optional parameters (e.g., n optional parameters). in such cases, i would need to write 2ⁿ search fn or search fn pattern to cover all scenarios, which is extremely inefficient and unscalable. questions: 1. is there any syntax to perform fuzzy matching for this scenario, so that both cases (with/without optional parameters passed) can be matched with a single pattern? 2. what is the difference between the two writing methods in the matching process: 1.callfunction(offset=keywordarg('offset')) 2.callfunction(keywordarg('offset'))? cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: torch.func transforms silently return zeros under inference mode
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] silently produce incorrect results (zeros) when called under [inline code] . no error is raised. [code block] root cause functorch transforms set up their own autograd context on entry: enable grad() for grad/vjp, set fwd grad enabled(true) for jvp (see note [grad and vjp interaction with no grad]). however, inference mode also excludes autograd dispatch keys from the tls dispatch key set, and the mode-enabling calls don't undo that. so autograd dispatch keys remain excluded and differentiation silently produces zeros. the transforms already correctly handle no grad — this is the same class of issue with a different tls mechanism. expected behavior either produce correct results (by also disabling inference mode on transform entry, matching the existing no grad handling) or raise an error. cc

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug issue description when compiling a function that performs a round-trip type conversion (fp32 → bfloat16 → fp32) using [inline code] , the output differs from the eager mode execution. reproduction code [code block] the assertion fails with a mismatch between eager and compiled outputs. generated triton kernel i tried to disable the registered replace pattern [inline code] in [url] and get the following generated triton kernel: [code block] the generated kernel completely skips the fp32 → bf16 → fp32 conversion chain. it directly loads fp32 values and stores them back as fp32, which is inconsistent with eager mode. while the inductor's optimization might produce numerically better results, we have a use case that requires strict bitwise equivalence with eager mode. is there a way to disable this specific optimization or enforce that type casts are preserved in the generated code? cc -chen -nrv versions torch 2.9.1

clean_title: lstm onnx files exports do not load in openvino
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug openvino generates errors when importing files generated by torch.onnx.export when lstms are used. i believe the problem is due to the input state slicing, which specifies the starts as 0 (scalar value), ends as 1 (scalar values) but the axes as the vector [0]. i think the starts/ends should also be (1d) vectors. if i hack the onnx model model.graph.initializer .dims = [1], then it works. i'm not sure what code even generates these initializers, so i can't pinpoint it any better. versions cc

clean_title: [torch.compile] massive numerical discrepancy ( 0.05 max abs error) in conv+bn+silu fusion with inductor fallback random=1
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using torch.compile with the inductor backend and enabling inductor fallback random=1, there is a significant numerical divergence compared to eager mode. in a standalone reproduction involving the first three blocks of a yolov8-like architecture (conv + bn + silu), the max absolute error reaches 0.05, and the max relative error exceeds 10^5. this suggests an unstable numerical approximation within the fused kernels. observed results: comparing mode a (eager execution) vs mode b (compiled with inductor fallback random=1): max absolute error: 5.4588e-02 max relative error: 1.0962e+05 rel l2 error (global): 3.8443e-04 eager output range: [-0.2785, 97.1013] compiled output range: [-0.2785, 97.0889] why this matters: while a 0.05 absolute error might seem small in isolation, this discrepancy is roughly 4 orders of magnitude higher than the expected numerical noise for fp32 fusion (typically < 1e-6). in full-scale yolov8 models, i have observed this error accumulating across layers, eventually reaching a max absolute error of 0.4 to 0.6, which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability. reproduction script: this script is truly standalone and does not require any external weights or data files. it manually initializes the weights to ensure intermediate activations reach a magnitude that exposes the numerical instability. clean_title: o(n^2) in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass
clean_body: there is a o(n^2) loop in torchscript onnx exporter initializer deduplication pass, which dominates export time of large models, including most huggingface llms. (n: number of initializers) the quadraticity comes from all-to-all comparison between all initializers: [url] since 1) [inline code] is called once for each of n initializers, and 2) [inline code] is a vector of initializers, this loop is o(n^2). although [inline code] function often only compares tensor metadata (shape, dtype, etc) and not actually compare the values of the tensors if the metadata didn't match, this loop starts to dominates the export time of large models with many initializers. in my observation, 1k initializers seems to be the tipping point, which is the case for most llms. reproducible example [code block] output [code block] suggestion define [inline code] as [inline code] instead of [inline code] with a proper hash function. with this, the deduplication pass can be significantly optimized, possibly into o(n). these are the possible hash keys for hashing [inline code] : tensor metadata -- shape, dtype, strides, ... rationale: this will reduce the export time by a significant constant factor. however, the time complexity remains o(n^2) if the number of tensors with same shape and dtype increases with n (which is true in llms) first element of the tensor rationale: most tensors differ from one another from the very first element. with a practical assumption that $p(x 1 = y 1 \rightarrow x = y) \approx 1$ , this will optimize the deduplication pass into o(n) in most practical cases. cc -nrv

clean_title: [numerical consistency] significant drift between eager and torch.compile triggered by inductor shape padding=1 on conv2d(1x1)
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i observed a significant numerical discrepancy ($ 10^{-3}$ max absolute error) between pytorch eager mode and torch.compile when testing a standard 1x1 convolution. the issue is specifically triggered or amplified when the optimization flag inductor shape padding=1 is enabled. why this matters: this level of error ($10^{-3}$) is unacceptable for a single layer in a deep network. in architectures like mobilenet or resnet, such numerical drift in early layers can accumulate and propagate through dozens of subsequent layers, eventually leading to inconsistent top-1 classification results or training divergence. the promise of torch.compile is bit-wise or near bit-wise consistency, which is broken here. clean_title: numerical discrepancy in nn.embedding between eager and compile modes

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when a dtensor with shard(dim) placement on a dynamically-sized dimension is redistributed to replicate, the gathered dimension's symbolic shape becomes n ceil(s/n) instead of s. this corrupted expression propagates through all downstream operations (view, transpose, reshape, to local), causing shape mismatches in compilation pipelines. root cause: shard. to replicate tensor in placement types.py 1. torch.chunk uses ceiling division: local size = (s + n - 1) // n 2. all gather concatenates n chunks: gathered = n ((s + n - 1) // n) 3. maybe unpad tensor checks "s % n != 0" — evaluates false for even s, so no unpadding occurs 4. result has symbolic size n ceil(s/n) ≠ s (sympy cannot simplify) reproduction run: [inline code] with the following ode as clean_title: [release 2.11][vllm] distributed failures - free memory on device cuda:0 (19.77/22.05 gib) on startup is less than desired gpu memory utilization (0.9, 19.84 gib)
clean_body: 24.02.2026: [url] analysis: title: pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0 increases baseline gpu memory usage, breaking memory-tight workloads environment: - pytorch 2.11.0+cu130 - cuda 13.0 / driver 570.133.20 - nvidia l4 (22.05 gib) - ray distributed executor - vllm (pipeline parallel test) description: after upgrading to pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0, the baseline gpu memory consumed before any model loading has increased compared to previous pytorch/cuda versions. on an nvidia l4 (22.05 gib total), only 19.77 gib is free after cuda context initialization, whereas previously enough memory was available for a 90% utilization target (19.84 gib). the 70 mib increase in baseline memory usage causes workloads that target gpu memory utilization=0.9 to fail with an oom-style error during device initialization, before any model weights are loaded. this occurs in a ray distributed setup (2 gpus, pp=2) running microsoft/phi-3.5-moe-instruct with dummy weights. the same configuration worked on prior pytorch + cuda versions. expected behavior: baseline cuda context memory consumption should not increase significantly between pytorch releases, or at minimum should be documented as a known change. reproduction: initialize cuda on an l4 gpu with pytorch 2.11+cu130 via ray workers and check torch.cuda.mem get info() — free memory is lower than with pytorch 2.10 or earlier. [code block] cc

clean_title: masked scatter flattens the source array on mps (apple silicon)
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug code behavior is different between mps and cpu/cuda. the bug is only relevant to mps device (apple silicon). tensor.masked scatter (mask, source) copies elements from source into self tensor at positions where the mask is true. elements from source are copied into self starting at position 0 of source and continuing in order one-by-one for each occurrence of mask being true. the shape of mask must be [broadcastable]( [url] with the shape of the underlying tensor. the source should have at least as many elements as the number of ones in mask. a minimal example to reproduce a bug: [code block] expected behavior: the behavior of 'masked scatter' is consistent across different accelerator devices. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: none rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: macos 26.3 (arm64) gcc version: could not collect clang version: 17.0.0 (clang-1700.6.3.2) cmake version: version 4.2.3 libc version: n/a python version: 3.10.19 (main, feb 4 2026, 00:02:50) [clang 21.1.4 ] (64-bit runtime) python platform: macos-26.3-arm64-arm-64bit is cuda available: false cuda runtime version: no cuda cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: no cuda nvidia driver version: no cuda cudnn version: no cuda is xpu available: false hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: true caching allocator config: n/a cpu: apple m4 pro versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] numpy==2.2.6 [pip3] torch==2.7.0 [pip3] torchaudio==2.7.0 [pip3] torchmetrics==1.8.2 [pip3] torchvision==0.22.0 [conda] could not collect cc

clean_body: the test [inline code] in [inline code] fails with [xpass(strict)] on cuda because of the xfail. test class : inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py::testgpuwrapper::test mm plus mm3 gpu wrapper platform : cuda error : xpass(strict) - test was expected to fail but passes steps to reproduce 1. build pytorch with cuda support. 2. run: [inline code] 4. observe [inline code] failing with [xpass(strict)] because it passes but is marked as expected to fail. root cause pr [ ]( [url] added an xfail on [inline code] in [inline code] with the reason "c++ wrapper dynamic shapes fails on cuda, fixed on rocm." on cuda, the test [inline code] actually passes, so it fails with [inline code] error. only the dynamic shapes variant [inline code] fails on cuda. so the xfail is too broad: it marks both tests as expected to fail when only the dynamic shapes run fails. proposed fix: remove the xfail from [inline code] and skip the dynamic shapes variant on cuda in [inline code] (similar to test mm plus mm2 dynamic shapes). so that the [inline code] test runs and passes on cuda. the dynamic shapes test is skipped on cuda (where it fails) and runs on rocm (where it passes). hence, for [inline code] , if we remove the xfail on the test and use a skip in [inline code] the dynamic variant which is known to fail on cuda is skipped and the xpass issue will be resolved. i have a fix for this and if this is a good approach i can go ahead with this. code references base test : test/inductor/test select algorithm.py:299 (test mm plus mm3) gpu wrapper config : test/inductor/test gpu cpp wrapper.py:107-125 (test failures gpu wrapper) cc: -xq -turnansky cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: flexattention does not allow captured tensor of ndim 0
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i am not sure this is a bug, or simply something the docs need to be more clear about. i am trying to implement an inference variant of scaled dot product attention where the key-value cache slots can be in any order (this is needed for sparse attention, where earlier slots can be overwritten by later ones). to get the right causal attention mask, i either need to (1) reorder the slots explicitly, or (2) define the causal attention mask implicitly via the token position vector. the latter would be faster and not need another copy of the kv cache. i tried to implement (2) with [inline code] . here is the code: [debug flex attention simple.py]( [url] as you can see, this works for variant (1), but gives me wrong results for variant (2). [code block] i don't understand how [inline code] is done inside. i suspect you simply do not support having a captured tensor as input which has [inline code] . things work fine with [inline code] as captured tensor, but not with [inline code] . in that case, it would be good if you could update your docs, stating that anything going into [inline code] must be a scalar or a scalar tensor. this is sad, because (2) would really save me some memory. versions collecting environment information... pytorch version: 2.10.0+cu128 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: 12.8 rocm used to clean_title: [release 2.11][vllm] quantization - test pre quantized model, torchao
clean_body: ci: [url] - 19.02.2026 test pre quantized model summary: the error: assert config.version == 2, f"unexpected version: {config.version}" assertionerror: unexpected version: 1 the model drisspg/fp8-opt-125m was quantized with an older torchao that used float8 config version 1, but the torchao version in your torch 2.11 image expects version 2. this is a breaking change in torchao's float8 dynamic activation float8 weight quantize tensor. the flow is: 1. torchaoconfig.from config detects is checkpoint torchao serialized = true (the model has quant method: torchao) 2. during create weights, it calls torchao quantize param data → quantize () to pre-quantize weights 3. torchao's quantize () hits the version assertion because the model's config is v1 this isn't something fixable in vllm — the model checkpoint itself is incompatible with the newer torchao. the options are: 1. re-quantize the model with the new torchao and push an updated drisspg/fp8-opt-125m 2. skip the test for this torch version (if this is a ci concern) 3. use a different fp8 test model that was quantized with the newer torchao [code block] cc

clean_title: ub in gemmhelper: vector::reserve() followed by operator[] access
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] in [inline code] does: [code block] reserve(1) allocates capacity but keeps size() at 0. accessing [0] is undefined behavior. with libc++ hardening ( libcpp hardening mode extensive), operator[] includes bounds checking and calls builtin trap() on violation, producing sigtrap at runtime. fix: reserve(1) → resize(1). the same pattern existed in aten/src/aten/native/mps/operations/shape.mm (inputtensors .reserve) but was incidentally eliminated by the refactor in . versions found by code inspection. present in current main (as of a79cc95) and all releases through v2.10.0. cc -arm

clean_title: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group
clean_body: hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang

clean_title: torch.nn.functional.scaled mm confusing use of lists/no validation before they are used
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] allows lists for swizzle type as well as allowing a default for [inline code] of [inline code] which translates into an empty list, but doesn't seem to check the lengths of the swizzle type lists before they are used. the default value of [inline code] is [inline code] in the [inline code] python api. this gets converted to an empty list [here]( [url] and this would lead to an invalid access of the first element of the empty list as above this leads to an error if the default is left as is. (i would have also expected [inline code] to be equivalent to [inline code] , which might make that enum value redundant?) [inline code] isn't used at all by gemm impls that aren't mxfp8/nvfp4, but there aren't any checks to ensure that no swizzle type was passed. only the first element is ever used for [inline code] , so passing a list with more elements is silently ignored regardless of gemm implementation used. versions affects the main branch at time of writing: [url] cc

clean_title: pytorch has conflict with orange pi3 / arm8 & numpy version / not available errors - can this be fixed please ?
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug many users are complaining here in the github issues and across the internet (besides me), - about core crashes (first time in at least a decade -since i saw a linux core crash, and the first time on my many pi boards - thx ! ) - and, the numpy not available error (compatibility ?) issue orange pi 3 (or generally < orange pi5) of : - illegal instruction call - core dump - numpy not error available i tried reverting to different versions of numpy, but could not avoid this problem. supposedly there was an earlier version of this that was stable on the orange pi 3's. maybe instructions / limits could be given / set for this ... this seems like a rampant problem, of inompatibility and core dump issues present in this branch of code, not normal to ubuntu / linux updates. can this be looked into - and correctly coded / patched - please ... ? this is not something that i would be proud of in my decades of coding standards, and definitely would not leave it hanging like this ... versions orange pi 3

clean_title: rtx 5060 (sm 120): pre-compiled cubins fail on windows despite being present in torch cuda.dll
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug pytorch cuda kernels fail with [inline code] on an nvidia geforce rtx 5060 (sm 120, blackwell) on windows, despite sm 120 cubins being confirmed present in [inline code] via [inline code] . all memory/driver operations succeed. only kernel launches fail. cupy 13.6.0 (which uses nvrtc runtime compilation) executes gpu kernels perfectly on the same system, proving the hardware and cuda driver are fully functional. diagnostic evidence what works: | operation | result | | [inline code] | [inline code] | | [inline code] | [inline code] | | [inline code] | [inline code] | | [inline code] | includes [inline code] | | [inline code] | pass | | [inline code] | pass | | [inline code] | pass | | [inline code] | pass | | cupy [inline code] | pass (gpu kernels work via nvrtc) | what fails: | operation | result | | [inline code] | fail - no kernel image | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] | fail | | [inline code] (cuda tensors) | fail | | [inline code] | fail - cublas status execution failed | cuobjdump confirms sm 120 cubins exist: [code block] cublas64 12.dll also contains sm 120 cubins. versions tested all fail identically: - [inline code] (stable) - [inline code] (stable) - [inline code] (stable) nvidia drivers tested both fail identically: - driver 576.52 (cuda 12.9) - driver 591.86 (cuda 13.1) environment - gpu: nvidia geforce rtx 5060 8gb (pny dual fan, desktop) - os: windows 11 (build 26200.7705) - cpu: intel core i7-14700 - python: 3.12.10 - cuda toolkit: 12.8 installed locally (not required by pytorch but tested) - cupy: 13.6.0 with cupy-cuda12x — works perfectly on same gpu reproduction steps [code block] key insight the cubins are present in the dll but fail to load/execute . this is not a missing kernel issue — it appears to be a cubin format or loading issue specific to windows builds. runtime-compiled kernels (via cupy/nvrtc) work perfectly, confirming the gpu hardware and cuda driver stack are functional. impact this completely blocks gpu acceleration for applications using pytorch on rtx 5060 on windows (e.g., setiastro suite pro for astrophotography image processing). users who purchased rtx 5060 gpus specifically for pytorch-based gpu compute cannot use them at all. related issues - (official support for sm 120 in stable builds) - (add official support for cuda sm 120) - nvidia developer forum: [pytorch can't run on 5060]( [url] — same exact issue, unresolved cc

clean_title: avoid cuda sync in spectral decompositions
clean_body: 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch current docs of spectral decompositions say that they require a device < -- cpu sync. i don't know if it's because of the sync, but in practice i am able to compute spectral decompositions for a batch of matrices using cupy interop via dlpack approximatley 10 times faster than with pytorch - and this includes a custom autograd function that also implements backprop for me (500x100x100 tensor, of 500 matrices of size 100x100). i am assuming that if cupy does it quickly, then nvidia already provided some primitives to do it quickly. so i am asking, if it's possible, to use them in pytorch internally as well. alternatives continue using cupy/dlpack interop. additional context i am working on researching trainable models whose nonlinear activations are matrix eigenvalues. making it fast will significantly accelerate my experiments without any special cupy hacks. cc

clean_title: torch.compile mode='reduce-overhead' regression: 7x slower cudagraphlaunch and new cudagraph trees.py overhead in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] has a 40-55% throughput regression in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9 on an h100 gpu running a 6b parameter production gpt inference engine. the root cause is 7x slower [inline code] calls and new python-level overhead in [inline code] that does not exist in 2.9. we performed a controlled isolation experiment with three configurations to separate the pytorch code regression from the cuda toolkit change, and found both pytorch 2.10's inductor code and the cuda 13.x toolkit contribute , with pytorch 2.10's code changes being the primary factor. environment - gpu : nvidia h100 80gb hbm3 (modal cloud) - pytorch versions tested : 2.9.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu130 - cuda toolkits tested : 12.8, 13.0/13.1 - os : ubuntu 24.04 (container) - python : 3.10 - workload : 6b parameter gpt model, [inline code] , 160 concurrent requests, batched inference throughput regression three-way isolation experiment (4 rounds x 500 steps, round 0 discarded, 3 scored) | configuration | med tok/s | stddev | cv | regression | | torch 2.9.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 8,016 | 160 | 2.0% | baseline | | torch 2.10.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 6,639 | 474 | 7.1% | -17.2% | | torch 2.10.0+cu130 (cuda 13.0) | 4,792 | 233 | 4.9% | -40.2% | the isolation experiment (same cuda 12.8, different pytorch) confirms pytorch 2.10's code is responsible for at least 17% throughput loss independent of cuda version. per-round stability | config | r0 (discard) | r1 | r2 | r3 | | torch 2.9 + cuda 12.8 | 8,122 | 7,873 | 8,189 | 7,984 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 12.8 | 7,782 | 7,129 | 6,606 | 6,182 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 13.1 | 5,313 | 5,046 | 4,743 | 4,588 | notable : both torch 2.10 configs show round-over-round degradation (declining throughput over sequential measurement windows) that torch 2.9 does not exhibit. this suggests state accumulation in the cuda graph tree management code. root cause: [inline code] + [inline code] perfetto traces captured with [inline code] show: 1. [inline code] is 7x slower per call | metric | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 41.25 ms | 293.27 ms | +611% | | [inline code] per call | 0.84 ms | 5.99 ms | +7.1x | | launch count | 49 | 49 | same | | graph instantiations | 1 | 1 | same | all 49 launches shifted from the [0.5-1.0) ms range (96% in 2.9) to the [5.0-10.0) ms range (100% in 2.10). 2. new python overhead in [inline code] | component | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 758 ms | 2,866 ms | +278% | | [inline code] avg/event | 0.124 ms | 0.471 ms | +280% | | [inline code] avg | 4.41 ms | 7.02 ms | +59% | 3. functions that appear in 2.10 but not in 2.9 traces | function | count | total time | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.76 ms | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.36 ms | these are entirely new overhead that did not exist in the 2.9 code path. what is not the cause - graph structure : same number of graphs (1), same launch count (49), same instantiation count (1) - gpu kernels : the actual cuda kernels (gemm, attention, triton fusions) are slightly faster in 2.10 - cuda toolkit alone : keeping cuda 12.8 while upgrading to torch 2.10 still shows 17% regression - compile mode : tried [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] — none recover performance. the [inline code] baseline remains the best strategy for both versions. reproducer the workload is a production gpt model, but the issue should be reproducible with any model using [inline code] with cuda graphs. the key characteristics: [code block] expected behavior [inline code] should take roughly the same time per call in 2.10 as in 2.9 ( 0.84ms), and the new [inline code] code paths should not add 667ms of python overhead per 50 inference steps. additional context - the round-over-round degradation pattern (throughput declining across sequential measurement rounds without container restart) in 2.10 but not 2.9 suggests the cuda graph tree management may be accumulating state or doing increasing work per replay - we have full perfetto traces for both versions available on request - our production system serves 1m+ dau and we are blocked from upgrading to 2.10 due to this regression cc

clean_title: armv8.1 lse regression for armv8.0 devices
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug pytorch 2.10.0 crashes on armv8.0 processors (cortex-a53/a72/a73) due to lse (large system extensions) instructions in libc10.so. this is a regression - issue reported this problem and claimed it was fixed in 2.4.1, but the issue has returned in 2.10.0. environment • hardware: arm cortex-a53 (armv8.0 architecture) • cpu features: fp asimd evtstrm aes pmull sha1 sha2 crc32 cpuid (notably missing lse) • os: debian trixie (testing), linux 6.12.63 • architecture: aarch64 (arm64) • pytorch version: 2.10.0 • installation method: pip (pip install torch) • python version: 3.13 error description any attempt to import or use pytorch results in sigill (illegal instruction) due to lse atomic instructions that are not supported on armv8.0 processors. error message illegal instruction (core dumped) gdb backtrace program received signal sigill, illegal instruction. 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so (gdb) bt 0x0000ffffbe27f3c8 in mi options init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe2755d0 in mi process init () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbe27560c in mi process load () from /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so 0x0000ffffbf7df180 in call init (env= , argv= , argc= , l= ) at ./elf/dl-init.c:74 call init (l= , argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:26 0x0000ffffbf7df2b8 in dl init (main map=0xffffbf7ff040, argc=1, argv=0xfffffffff548, env=0xfffffffff558) at ./elf/dl-init.c:121 0x0000ffffbf7fa660 in dl start user () from /lib/ld-linux-aarch64.so.1 disassembly at crash point (gdb) x/5i $pc = 0xffffbe27f3c8 < mi options init+1064 : ldaddal x1, x2, [x0] ; ← lse instruction (sigill) 0xffffbe27f3cc < mi options init+1068 : cmp x2, x0 0xffffbe27f3d0 < mi options init+1072 : b.ne 0xffffbe27f014 < mi options init+100 0xffffbe27f3d4 < mi options init+1076 : adrp x0, 0xffffbe465000 0xffffbe27f3d8 < mi options init+1080 : add x0, x0, x9c8 the ldaddal instruction is part of the armv8.1 lse extension and is not supported on armv8.0 processors like cortex-a53. analysis lse instruction count in libc10.so $ objdump -d /usr/local/lib/python3.13/dist-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so | \ grep -e 'ldadd|ldclr|ldeor|ldset|swp|cas' | wc -l 22 the library contains 22 lse instructions, making it completely incompatible with armv8.0 processors. root cause: mimalloc dependency the crash occurs in mi options init() which is part of mimalloc (microsoft's memory allocator). pytorch's c10 library links against mimalloc-static: from c10/cmakelists.txt: if(use mimalloc) target link libraries(c10 private mimalloc-static) endif() it appears that the mimalloc build included in the pytorch wheel is compiled with lse instructions enabled, likely via -march=armv8.1-a or -march=armv8-a+lse flags. steps to reproduce 1. use any armv8.0 processor (cortex-a53, a57, a72, a73 - common in raspberry pi 3/4, many sbcs, older servers) 2. verify cpu lacks lse: grep -o 'atomics\|lse' /proc/cpuinfo (should return nothing) 3. install pytorch 2.10.0: pip install torch 4. try to import: python3 -c "import torch" 5. observe sigill crash regression information this is a regression from the fix mentioned in issue : • original issue: reported lse incompatibility on armv8.0 • claimed fix: said to be resolved in pytorch 2.4.1 • regression: the problem has returned in pytorch 2.10.0 the lse instructions should not be present in builds targeting general arm64 compatibility. the standard -march=armv8-a (without +lse) should be used for maximum compatibility. versions collecting environment information... pytorch version: n/a is debug build: n/a cuda used to build pytorch: n/a rocm used to build pytorch: n/a os: debian gnu/linux 13 (trixie) (aarch64) gcc version: (debian 14.2.0-19) 14.2.0 clang version: could not collect cmake version: version 3.31.6 libc version: glibc-2.41 python version: 3.13.5 (main, jun 25 2025, 18:55:22) [gcc 14.2.0] (64-bit runtime) python platform: linux-6.12.63+deb13-arm64-aarch64-with-glibc2.41 is cuda available: n/a cuda runtime version: could not collect cuda module loading set to: n/a gpu models and configuration: could not collect nvidia driver version: could not collect cudnn version: could not collect is xpu available: n/a hip runtime version: n/a miopen runtime version: n/a is xnnpack available: n/a caching allocator config: n/a cpu: architecture: aarch64 cpu op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit byte order: little endian cpu(s): 4 on-line cpu(s) list: 0-3 vendor id: arm bios vendor id: arm limited model name: cortex-a53 bios model name: cpu @ 0.0ghz bios cpu family: 2 model: 4 thread(s) per core: 1 core(s) per cluster: 4 socket(s): 1 cluster(s): 1 stepping: r0p4 cpu(s) clean_title: cuda error on rtx 5050: no kernel image is available for execution on the device
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i recently purchased an nvidia rtx 50xx (blackwell architecture) gpu. when running any pytorch cuda operation, i get the following error: cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device this happens even with the simplest cuda test, and also when trying to run stable diffusion (comfyui / a1111). it seems pytorch does not yet include compiled cuda kernels for blackwell gpus. please add official support for nvidia blackwell architecture as soon as possible. versions pytorch version: latest stable (pip install torch) cuda version: 12.x (from nvidia driver) nvidia driver version: 591.74 gpu: nvidia rtx 5050 (blackwell) os: windows 11 cc

clean_title: triu tril kernel causes hsa status error memory aperture violation on amd mi210/mi300x with fp16 tensors
clean_body: triu tril kernel causes hsa status error memory aperture violation on amd mi210/mi300x with fp16 tensors 🐛 describe the bug the [inline code] causes an illegal memory access ( [inline code] ) on amd instinct mi210 and mi300x gpus when computing triangular operations on fp16 tensors. this commonly manifests when running transformer models that create causal attention masks. environment - pytorch version : 2.11.0.dev20260118+rocm7.1 - rocm version : 7.1.52802 - gpus tested : - amd instinct mi210 (gfx90a) - 2x 64gb configuration - amd instinct mi300x (gfx942) - os : linux (ubuntu) - python : 3.12 - how pytorch was installed : pip (rocm nightly wheels) error message [code block] minimal reproducer (pure pytorch) [code block] reproducer with huggingface (real-world use case) the bug was originally discovered when running the opt model for perplexity evaluation: [code block] analysis the demangled kernel name is: [code block] this indicates: - data type : [inline code] (fp16) - index type : [inline code] - operation : [inline code] (template param = true) - vector width : 4 - unknown param : false the issue appears to be in the hip implementation of the triangular kernel when: 1. using fp16 dtype 2. operating on larger tensors (2048x2048 in the attention mask case) 3. the grid size [inline code] suggests a 1d kernel launch for the flattened tensor expected behavior the [inline code] operation should complete without memory access violations, producing a valid upper triangular matrix. additional context - the issue does not occur on nvidia gpus with the same code and similar pytorch versions - using [inline code] instead of sdpa does not help - the error happens early in inference (during the first few forward passes) - both mi210 (gfx90a) and mi300x (gfx942) architectures are affected - the issue was discovered during gptq paper reproduction experiments on amd hardware potential causes 1. out-of-bounds memory access in the hip kernel when computing thread indices 2. incorrect grid/block dimension calculations for the hip backend 3. memory alignment issues specific to the gfx90a/gfx942 architectures 4. issue with the vectorized fp16 path (vector width = 4) workaround attempts 1. using [inline code] - did not help 2. reducing sequence length - may help in some cases but not a real solution 3. using fp32 instead of fp16 - untested, would defeat the purpose of quantization experiments related this may be related to other rocm memory issues but i could not find an existing issue tracking this specific kernel crash. cc -amd (rocm maintainers) --- labels suggestion : module: rocm, module: cuda, triaged

clean_title: [heads-up] pytorch 2.9.x conv3d regression may significantly slow qwen3-vl vision forward
clean_body: this is not a qwen3-vl implementation bug, but an upstream pytorch behavior that may affect qwen3-vl users. we observed that upgrading pytorch from 2.8.x to 2.9.x can cause a 10–20× slowdown in vision-conditioned forward / generation for qwen3-vl, while text-only / llm-only inference remains unaffected. root cause (analysis) qwen3-vl vision patch embedding uses a conv3d layer (model.visual.patch embed.proj). on pytorch 2.9.1 (cuda 12.8), this conv3d may fall back to aten::slow conv dilated3d for certain shapes / gpus, instead of using a fast cudnn kernel. profiling shows this single conv3d dominates the entire vision forward. this behavior has been reported upstream in pytorch and appears to be resolved in pytorch 2.10. practical suggestions - if you see severe vision-side slowdowns, consider staying on pytorch 2.8.x - or upgrade to pytorch 2.10+ - be cautious when benchmarking qwen3-vl vision performance on pytorch 2.9.x posting this as a heads-up so users are aware of the upstream interaction. cc

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug i encounter a cuda error when running stable diffusion / comfyui on a new nvidia gpu. error message: cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device environment: - os: windows 10 - gpu: nvidia (new generation, recently released) - cuda: installed and detected - pytorch: installed with cuda support the same setup works in cpu mode, but fails immediately when using gpu. this appears to be related to missing cuda kernel support for this gpu architecture in the current pytorch build. please let me know if support for this gpu architecture is planned, or if there is a recommended nightly build or workaround versions not available yet. i am not able to run collect env.ru because cuda execution fails on this gpu. cpu mode works correctly. cc

clean_title: [inline code] operators silently broken with [inline code]
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using the [inline code] operators on mps, pytorch raises an exception that they are not implemented, suggesting to use the cpu fallback with [inline code] . however, the [inline code] operators are silently broken when using the cpu fallback, as shown by this reproduction case: [code block] if you run [inline code] , the assertion will fail on every rank except 0, because [inline code] didn't actually modify [inline code] . initially, it seems like this could be fixed by adding mutable aliases to the schema for [inline code] , so [inline code] knows to copy back to the input tensors: [url] however, this does not work as the [inline code] operators can be async: 1. [inline code] has already copied from the cpu tensor back to mps by the time it returns, before the async op is complete 2. the result stored in the returned [inline code] will be the cpu tensor in fact, this does not even reliably work for [inline code] with gloo, since gloo does all operations async and relies on python to handle [inline code] : [url] here's a reproduction case for [inline code] that more clearly demonstrates what's going on: [code block] potential fix a potential fix would be to: 1. add mutable aliases to the schemas for all the [inline code] operators 2. have [inline code] handle [inline code] by wrapping it to do the copies (and return the original tensor) once the original [inline code] is complete however, this is a lot of complexity that probably isn't worth it -- more likely, [inline code] should reject the [inline code] operators (and ideally gloo could natively support mps 🙂) versions [code block] cc -huang

clean_body: 🐛 bug report description the new [inline code] exporter fails when models use custom [inline code] classes that access [inline code] in the backward pass. this is a blocker for exporting video restoration models (like darkir, restoreformer, etc.) that use custom normalization functions for performance optimization. error [code block] minimal reproducible example [code block] expected behavior custom autograd functions with [inline code] or [inline code] should be exportable, or a clear workaround should be documented. actual behavior dynamo exporter raises [inline code] --- 📋 environment - pytorch version: 2.5.1 (also tested with 2.9.1) - python version: 3.12.10 - onnx version: 1.20.1 - onnxscript version: 0.5.7 - os: windows 11 - cuda: 11.8 / 12.1 --- 🔍 context why this matters many video restoration and computer vision models use custom autograd functions for: - performance optimization (faster backward pass) - numerical stability (custom gradient computation) - memory efficiency (selective tensor saving) examples of affected models: - darkir (cvpr 2025) - video restoration - restoreformer - face restoration - basicvsr++ - video super-resolution - many models with custom layernorm, instancenorm, or attention mechanisms related issues - ✅ resolved: []( [url] - fft operations now supported in dynamo exporter (pytorch 2.6+) - ❓ this issue: custom autograd functions not supported the fft issue was resolved, but custom autograd remains a blocker for onnx export. --- 💡 possible solutions option 1: add support for [inline code] allow dynamo to handle [inline code] / [inline code] unpacking in backward passes. option 2: provide workaround documentation document how to rewrite custom autograd functions for dynamo compatibility: - use standard pytorch ops only - avoid manual [inline code] / [inline code] - show examples of equivalent implementations option 3: fallback to torchscript for custom ops allow dynamo exporter to fall back to torchscript export for submodules with custom autograd, similar to how it handles unsupported ops. --- 🎯 requested action 1. investigate if custom autograd function support can be added to dynamo exporter 2. document workarounds if architectural limitations prevent support 3. provide guidance on rewriting custom functions for onnx compatibility --- 📎 additional information full stack trace click to expand full traceback [code block] workaround attempts ❌ tried: using [inline code] instead of [inline code] - same error ❌ tried: [inline code] the custom function - still fails in dynamo ✅ working: pytorch jit compilation (torchscript) - but no npu/directml support 🤝 contribution happy to provide more test cases or help with documentation if needed! use case: video restoration application for vhs/analog tape digitization - need npu/directml support for efficient batch processing. --- cc

clean_title: missing sha256 hashes for certain artifacts on index
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug hi 👋 , the same issue was reported and fixed a few days ago in [url] for cpu builds of version = 2.9.1 but i noticed it's also the case for xpu builds and a few more are missing as well. i think the following script should list most of the affected urls that don't have a sha256 checksum: bash for index in cu126 cu128 cu130 xpu rocm7.1 rocm7.0 rocm6.4 ; do echo "index $index:" ; curl -s " [url] \ | grep -op ' = 2.9.1 cc

clean_title: [tooling] memory visualizer ( [url] zoom functionality broken: typeerror: cannot read properties of null (reading 'ctrlkey')
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug 🐛 describe the bug the pytorch memory visualizer ( [url] is currently unable to zoom or interact with the memory timeline. while the page loads after the recent cdn source change (related to ), any attempt to zoom triggers a javascript error in the console, rendering the tool partially unusable. is it a problem on my end or do other people also see a blank page there? 🚨 error stack trace [code block] 🕵️ possible cause (note: this technical analysis was suggested by an ai assistant.i'm not sure if it's helpful or correct) the error is a known breaking change when moving from d3 v5 to d3 v6/v7. the code in memory viz appears to rely on the global d3.event (specifically accessing d3.event.sourceevent.ctrlkey). in d3 v6+, d3.event is removed, causing it to be null when the zoom listener is triggered. it seems the recent fix for the skypack cdn failure might have inadvertently introduced a d3 v6+ environment or a mismatched d3-zoom module while the visualizer logic remains on v5. versions n/a cc

clean_title: inductor unit test fudge factors need readjustment
clean_body: in a recent [pr]( [url] i fixed (?) a precision issue of the math backend attention: in the last line of method [inline code] we can prop up the precision of the calculation by performing the last matrix multiply in higher precision: [code block] while it fixes my problem mentioned in the pr, lots of other unit test start to fail, but only because those unit tests assume that math backend attention at [inline code] (vs. [inline code] ) sucks (!) here are some results from a [standalone simplified repro]( [url] that mimicks the [inline code] test: [code block] in the high precision case the unit test fails, but not because paged attention [inline code] would suck, but because math backend [inline code] becomes awesome. so it defeats the purpose. i think all "fudge factors" should be re-adjusted to take account the new better math backend [inline code] performance. the ci test for the pr report failures for: mi200, mi300, but also for cpu . looking quickly into the failed tests, most of it seems to be about this: [code block] i.e. here the reference errors become smaller than before i believe. i'd need some confirmation: please confirm this effect .. am i missing/misunderstanding/fat-fingering something here? how should we proceed? we'll split the effort of modifying the fudge factors? appendum: for some dynamo benchmarks on nvidia the test fail because(?) of this: [code block] i.e., good news an example of a test that fails on nvidia: [code block] can't think of why the math backend attention would affect this one.. cc

clean_title: [dtensor]inconsistent random number generation in torch.distributed.tensor.randn
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug bug description: when using torch.distributed.tensor.randn to create dtensors with fixed random seeds, the generated tensors are inconsistent between single-gpu and multi-gpu scenarios. specifically, creating a (4, 4) tensor on a single gpu produces different results compared to creating (2, 4) tensors on two gpus separately, even when using the same random seed. expected behavior: with the same random seed, the distributed tensor creation should maintain consistency with single-device semantics. the concatenation of multi-gpu results should match the single-gpu result when using equivalent global shapes and seeds. actual behavior: the random values generated in distributed mode differ from single-device mode, breaking reproducibility and debugging capabilities. versions torch 2.9.1 cuda 12.4 python scripy [inline code] cc -huang -l

clean_title: seq faults after update [gfx1151]
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug about two weeks ago i updated pytorch with the nightly build that i usually do every week or two. my system was working fine, not a single issue besides the one-off quirks you usually see. but after the update, i found that comfyui was failing to load any models. at first i thought it was the comfyui update that broke it, so i started there, but couldn't find any issue. the only message it was giving me was a seg fault when trying to load the model. i then turned to pytorch and found that it would seg fault during a basic torch command. [code block] i've provided the valgrind, rocminfo, and boot entries. another odd thing is that rewinding the version, doesn't seem to fix the issue. i went back as far as a month and i got the exact same seg fault. another thing i should mention is that i did do a system update just before updating pytorch, so it could be possible that this could have happened from an amd driver update and looking in the wrong spot. if i was smart, i would have tested torch after doing the system update before updating torch, but i have done this so many times, that i got used to it just working. versions [collected env.txt]( [url] [rocminfo.txt]( [url] [valgrind results.txt]( [url] [boot options.txt]( [url] cc -amd

clean_title: triton wheels don't have hashes in urls
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug currently the triton wheels url format in the pytorch index doesn't include hashes in the same format as the wheels for torch. this results in tools like uv not storing hashes in the lockfile, which is a supply chain security concern. torch: [code block] triton: [code block] note that in the torch url the actual href ends with [inline code] , while that is missing from the triton urls. clearly in the triton case the sha is still computed since it's in these other metadata fields, but i guess that isn't part of the server spec (assuming uv is doing the right thing here). some of the torch urls don't have this, so maybe newer torch wheels have this issue too? more context from [pep503]( [url] the url should include a hash in the form of a url fragment with the following syntax: = , where is the lowercase name of the hash function (such as sha256) and is the hex encoded digest. versions current index cc

clean_title: vllm on cpu crashes on models re-runs with torch 2.10
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug while updating pytorch version in vllm from 2.9.1 to 2.10 we noticed that after the first run, vllm/pytorch would try to load the compiled model which does not happen successfully and causes the engine to crash. please see: [url] for reproducer and stack trace. we hot-fixed this in vllm by we temporarily disabling aot compile for cpus in [url] i'm raising this issue here for us to make sure that root cause is fixed. i'm still not sure if the fix should be in vllm or in pytorch versions torch 2.10, on aarch64/x86 cpus cc -arm

clean_title: torch.compile(dynamic=true) specializes batch dimension for layernorm backward, causing recompiles
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary: when using [inline code] with [inline code] to trace a module containing [inline code] and [inline code] , and then compiling the resulting fx graph with [inline code] , the batch dimension ( [inline code] ) gets specialized. this causes a new recompilation for every new batch size, triggered by a "guard size mismatch at index 0". this behavior is specific to the presence of the [inline code] operator in the graph. a forward-only [inline code] graph compiles with a truly dynamic batch dimension as expected. background: i am working on a machine learning force field project and aim to accelerate my model's training process using torch.compile. briefly, the machine learning force field model reads distance vectors between atoms and outputs energy and forces, where the latter are derived by computing the gradient of energy with respect to the distance vectors. in other words, the forward() function of my model contains manually invoked autograd operations. additionally, the loss function of my model consists of two components (energy and forces), which means that higher-order gradients need to be computed during the backward pass of training. meanwhile, the "batch size" varies for each batch (since the number of atoms in each molecule is different), which requires that the code generated by compilation does not statically fix the dimensions of the input tensors. to address this, i first traced my computation graph using make fx(mode="symbolic"), then compiled the model with torch.compile(dynamic=true). however, i found that the compiled output still has statically fixed dimensions, leading to frequent recompilation when feeding inputs of different shapes. after analysis, i identified that the root cause of the recompilation is the backward operator of torch.nn.layernorm. a minimal reproducible example is provided below: minimal reproducer: [code block] [tl out.zip]( [url] error logs expected behavior: with [inline code] , the compiled model should handle a dynamic batch dimension ( [inline code] ) without recompiling. the first run (e.g., [inline code] ) should produce a compiled artifact where the batch dimension is treated as a [inline code] . subsequent runs with different [inline code] values (20, 30, etc.) should reuse this artifact without triggering any guard failures related to the batch dimension. actual behavior: for each new batch size [inline code] , a recompilation is triggered. the logs show a series of guard failures indicating that the batch dimension was specialized to the previous value. for example, when running with [inline code] after running with [inline code] through [inline code] , the logs show: v0120... clean_title: [onnx] gridsample nodes lose all metadata props when exporting with dynamo=true and opset = 20
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when exporting a model with torch.onnx.export(..., dynamo=true), gridsample onnx nodes have no metadata props at all (no name scopes, class hierarchy, stack trace, etc.) when using opset = 20. the same export with opset 16-18 correctly propagates all metadata. this is a regression - the fx graph capture has full source information, but the onnx translation for aten.grid sampler → gridsample drops it for newer opsets. [code block] output [code block] expected behavior gridsample nodes should have metadata props (including pkg.torch.onnx.name scopes, pkg.torch.onnx.class hierarchy, pkg.torch.onnx.stack trace) for all supported opset versions, same as other ops like gemm. actual behavior opset 16, 17, 18: gridsample has full metadata ✅ opset 20, 22: gridsample has zero metadata props ❌ impact this breaks tooling that relies on onnx node metadata to map nodes back to pytorch module hierarchy (e.g., for profiling, debugging, visualization). additional context the report=true diagnostic output shows the fx graph has correct source annotations for grid sampler, but these are not propagated to the onnx gridsample nodes for opset = 20. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0+cu128 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: 12.8 cudnn version: 90701 python version: 3.10.11 (main, may 7 2023, 19:26:31) [clang 16.0.3] cuda available: true gpu: nvidia geforce rtx 4090 os: ubuntu 22.04.4 lts (x86 64) cc

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary i know [inline code] / [inline code] have been deprecated (and the warning in the logs makes that clear), so i’m mostly filing this as a “heads up” in case they’re still expected to degrade gracefully rather than segfault. the repro involves a tiny model with a registered buffer constant. export + aot compile succeed, but [inline code] fails during constant loading with: [code block] and then the process terminates with a segmentation fault. even for deprecated apis, this feels like it should probably error out cleanly instead of crashing. --- minimal reproduction [code block] --- actual output [code block] the stack trace shows the failure happening inside aotinductormodelbase constant loading, then a crash in the cuda path after [inline code] . --- expected behavior given that this api is deprecated, i wouldn’t expect correctness fixes or new features, but i would expect at least one of: a clean python exception, or a clear “unsupported” error, or even a graceful no-op fallback rather than a segfault. the crash path seems related to constants being treated as host pointers during aoti constant loading. this also repros even without backward/aotautograd complexity — just export → aot compile → aot load. the deprecation warning points to: [code block] which i’ll use going forward, but reporting the segfault just in case someone still depends on the old api for legacy workloads. versions [code block] cc -amd

clean_title: torch.compile fails with notimplementederror for mkldnn layout tensor: 'aten::addmm.out' not available for mkldnncpu backend
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary torch.compile raises notimplementederror when executing a compiled model with mkldnn-layout tensor input, while eager mode works correctly. the error occurs because aten::addmm.out is not implemented for the mkldnncpu backend. this prevents users from combining torch.compile optimization with mkldnn tensor format, which is a common use case for cpu performance optimization. root cause analysis the issue is a backend dispatch mismatch between eager and compiled execution: eager mode: when input is mkldnn layout, nn.linear dispatches to mkldnn. linear pointwise which handles mkldnn tensors correctly. compiled mode: inductor generates code using aten::addmm.out, which does not have an implementation for the mkldnncpu backend. the compiled graph should either preserve the mkldnn-specific operator dispatch or convert mkldnn tensors to dense format before using aten::addmm.out. expected vs actual behavior expected behavior: torch.compile should work with mkldnn tensor inputs (same as eager mode), or a clear error should be raised at compile time indicating that mkldnn tensors are not supported. actual behavior: eager mode: works correctly ✓ compiled mode: raises notimplementederror at runtime ✗ fuzzer compliance checklist this bug was discovered by fuzzing. topic: fuzzer (label request) rtol/atol: n/a (runtime crash, not numerical comparison). fp64 ref: n/a (execution crashes before output). fallback random=true: enabled in reproducer. rng seed reset: enabled in reproducer. minimal reproducer [code block] error logs === configuration === x dense: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false x mkldnn: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=true --- dense tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false --- dense tensor: torch.compile --- compiled out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false values match: true --- mkldnn tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=true --- mkldnn tensor: torch.compile --- notimplementederror: could not run 'aten::addmm.out' with arguments from the 'mkldnncpu' backend. this could be because the operator doesn't exist for this backend, or was omitted during the selective/custom build process (if using custom build). clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] produces non-zero gradients for zero-size groups (experts with no tokens) on b200 (blackwell, sm 100) gpus, while it correctly produces zero gradients on h800 (hopper, sm 90) gpus. this is a critical bug for mixture-of-experts (moe) models where some experts may have zero tokens assigned in certain batches. versions 🔍 expected behavior when a group has [inline code] (no tokens assigned to that expert), the weight gradient for that expert should be zero because there is no input to compute gradients from. ❌ actual behavior on b200 gpus, zero-size groups produce non-zero gradients with significant magnitude, indicating that the backward kernel is incorrectly computing gradients using uninitialized or invalid data. 📋 steps to reproduce 1. run the provided test script on a b200 gpu: [code block] 2. the script will show non-zero gradients for all zero-size groups. minimal reproducible example [code block] 🔧 environment b200 (bug present) - pytorch version : 2.9.1 - cuda version : 12.9 - compute capability : 10.0 (sm 100) - architecture : blackwell h800 (working correctly) - pytorch version : 2.9.1 - cuda version : 12.9 - compute capability : 9.0 (sm 90) - architecture : hopper 📊 test results [test grouped mm zero size bug.py]( [url] on b200 (bug): [code block] on h800 (correct): [code block] 💡 logs [blackwell.log]( [url] [hopper.log]( [url] cc

clean_body: 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch fixing this should help unblock motivations (as mentioned in ) some motivation for this: firstly, the desire to do basic data-dependent computation has come up several times, but one of the key use cases is skipping the optimizer in the case of a nan gradient in mixed precision training. secondly, figuring out making torch.cond() work with cuda graphs paves the way for adding support for torch.while loop() in cuda graphs, which may be used to do moe inside a cuda graph without dropping tokens, but that is more complicated and requires its own issue to write down those issues (they go beyond just while loop support for cuda graphs; it may also need tricky memory management for autograd). repro that should work with the fix: [code block] cc: alternatives no response additional context potential patch could look like this - [url] cc: for your thoughts cc
